analysis_summary: The proposed method produced 3 finding(s) from 6 analysis obligation(s).

findings.summary: jtag_unlock likely clears all locks without authentication because the condition does not include any hash check.
findings.vulnerability_category: Assert jtag_unlock = 1 to bypass all lock controls / reglk_ctrl_o (lock outputs) / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: jtag_unlock signal Operation: Assert jtag_unlock = 1 to bypass all lock controls Object: reglk_ctrl_o (lock outputs) Expected guard: jtag_unlock must be asserted only after successful hash authentication Observed behavior: When jtag_unlock=1, the reglk_mem reset condition (~(rst_ni && ~jtag_unlock && ~rst_9)) becomes true, forcing all reglk_mem entries to 'h0 (unlocked). The hash comparison logic inside dmi_jtag is not visible in the provided RTL. Path: jtag_unlock → reglk_wrapper.jtag_unlock → condition in always block clears reglk_mem → reglk_ctrl_o → lock bits to all peripherals Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: All peripheral locks are unconditionally cleared. If jtag_unlock can be asserted without proper authentication, the entire lock mechanism is bypassed.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Internal hash comparison logic of dmi_jtag is not inspectable; cannot verify if jtag_unlock requires a valid hash match.

findings.summary: The OR gate grants PKT access when HMAC is accessible, creating an unintended dependency.
findings.vulnerability_category: Read/Write to PKT peripheral / PKT wrapper (pkt_wrapper) / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: CPU access request to peripheral j=5 (PKT) Operation: Read/Write to PKT peripheral Object: PKT wrapper (pkt_wrapper) Expected guard: Access to peripheral 5 must be authorized solely by its own access control bit (acc_ctrl[5*4+i]) Observed behavior: acc_ctrl_c[i][5] = acc_ctrl[5*4+i] | acc_ctrl[4*4+i]. Therefore, if peripheral 4 (HMAC) access control bit is set, PKT is accessible even when its own access control bit is zero. Path: acc_ctrl_c computation (line 222) → used in pkt_wrapper en = en_acct && acct_ctrl_i (which is acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][5]) Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: PKT can be accessed without its own authorization, violating independent peripheral protection.

findings.summary: Reset default is insecure; peripherals are unlocked.
findings.vulnerability_category: After reset, before locks are configured / reglk_ctrl outputs and protected registers / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: System reset (rst_ni) Operation: After reset, before locks are configured Object: reglk_ctrl outputs and protected registers Expected guard: reglk_ctrl must reset to all zeros (locked) for secure default Observed behavior: reglk_mem resets to all zeros (0 = unlocked). acct_mem resets to all ones (full access). Therefore, after reset all peripherals are unlocked and fully accessible. Path: rst_ni deassertion → reglk_mem[j] <= 'h0 (in reglk_wrapper) → reglk_ctrl_o = 0 → lock bits = unlock; acct_mem[j] <= 32'hffffffff (full access) Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Peripherals are unprotected by default until software explicitly sets lock bits, violating secure-by-default principle.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Lock bit semantics (0=unlocked, 1=locked) confirmed in read/write gating of wrappers.

global_uncertainty: No closure-level missing evidence was recorded beyond the visible source scope limitations.
